(from [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language")  *rama* 'branch') is a temporary or permanent shelter in the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") equipped with a roof but no walls, or only partially enclosed.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Shelter with a roof and no walls, common in the southwestern U.S.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Ramada_(shelter) "Special:EditPage/Ramada (shelter)") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Ramada" shelter](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Ramada%22+shelter) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Ramada%22+shelter+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Ramada%22+shelter&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Ramada%22+shelter+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Ramada%22+shelter) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Ramada%22+shelter&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2007)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg/40px-Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg)

Look up ***[ramada](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ramada "wiktionary:ramada")*** in Wiktionary, the free dictionary.

For the hotel chain, see [Ramada](/wiki/Ramada "Ramada").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Casa_Grande_under_shelter.jpg/250px-Casa_Grande_under_shelter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_Grande_under_shelter.jpg)

The Olmsted ramada over the Big House of [Casa Grande National Monument](/wiki/Casa_Grande_National_Monument "Casa Grande National Monument") in [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona").

A **ramada** (from [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language")  *rama* 'branch') is a temporary or permanent shelter in the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") equipped with a roof but no walls, or only partially enclosed.

Ramadas have traditionally been constructed with branches or bushes by [indigenous Americans](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") living in the region. However, the term today is also applied to permanent concrete, wooden, or steel structures used to shelter objects or people from the sun. For example, public parks in desert areas of the United States may contain ramadas with picnic tables, restrooms, water sources, etc. Since sunlight is more of an environmental hazard than wind or snow or rain in this part of the world, a roof alone provides substantial shelter. And because there are no walls in the structure, airflow is unrestricted, helping the solar barrier keep the temperature below the roof somewhat cooler than ambient.

An example of a large modern-day ramada can be seen at the [Casa Grande Ruins National Monument](/wiki/Casa_Grande_Ruins_National_Monument "Casa Grande Ruins National Monument") in Arizona, where it is used to protect ancient ruins.
